title: Postinfectious Glomerulonephritis
buzzwords: Child with periorbital and peripheral edema
Nephritic syndrome with hypertension and hematuria
Urinalysis with dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts
Low serum C3 with normal C4
Subepithelial hump-shaped immune deposits on EM
Starry sky granular IgG/C3 weeks post-strep pharyngitis
transcript:
# Postinfectious Glomerulonephritis

## Overview

Postinfectious glomerulonephritis (PIGN) is a kidney injury characterized by glomerular inflammation. It usually presents as nephritic syndrome (hematuria, hypertension, edema) and most often arises in children weeks after a systemic infection outside the kidney. Streptococcus pyogenes (group A beta-hemolytic strep) is the classic cause, but staphylococcal infections are increasingly recognized, especially in adults. When caused by group A Streptococcus, the disease is called poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN). PIGN is classified as a secondary glomerular disease because it results from a systemic infection outside the kidney.

## Epidemiology

- Incidence is very low in developed countries; much more common in developing countries due to disparities in antibiotics, clean water, and sanitation.
- Globally, PIGN is the leading cause of acute glomerulonephritis in children.
- Most cases occur in children aged 5 to 12 years, though young adults are also affected.

## Clinical Presentation (LO #1)

- History of a recent infectious illness.
- With S. pyogenes: symptoms appear 1 to 3 weeks after pharyngitis, or 3 to 6 weeks after a strep skin infection (impetigo, cellulitis).
- Presentation ranges from asymptomatic microscopic hematuria to abrupt nephritic syndrome.
- Common signs/symptoms: gross hematuria, periorbital and peripheral edema, hypertension.
- Hematuria is often dark brown, described as tea-colored or cola-colored urine.
- Children classically appear suddenly swollen up overnight with dark urine.
- In staphylococcal PIGN, renal symptoms appear during the active infection rather than weeks later.

## Pathophysiology (LO #2)

- PIGN follows an extrarenal infection and damages the glomerulus through a type III hypersensitivity reaction.
- Infections generate IgG (or IgM) that form circulating immune complexes, which deposit in the glomerulus, particularly in subepithelial and mesangial locations along the glomerular basement membrane (GBM).
- Immune complexes activate complement, recruiting phagocytes and producing inflammation and tissue damage.
- Complement consumption (alternative pathway activation) leads to decreased serum C3.

### Bacteriology and Immunopathology

- Many pathogens can cause PIGN: bacteria (especially gram-positive staph and strep), viruses, parasites.
- Group A beta-hemolytic Streptococcus (S. pyogenes) is the classic cause; only certain strains have nephritogenic antigens.
- Streptococcal skin infections (impetigo, cellulitis) and pharyngitis can both trigger PSGN.
- Onset of renal disease is typically 1 to 6 weeks after S. pyogenes infection.
- Streptococcal infection can also lead to other immune-mediated disorders (acute rheumatic fever, scarlet fever).
- Staphylococcus-associated glomerulonephritis is increasingly seen in U.S. adults, often in hospitalized patients with serious infections of the heart (endocarditis), skin (cellulitis), bone (osteomyelitis), or lungs (pneumonia); renal disease appears within days, not weeks.
- Proposed mechanisms include molecular mimicry between bacterial antigens and host proteins, immune complex formation, and activation of autoantibodies.

## Histopathology

- Biopsy is not usually needed; reserved for atypical cases or severe disease.
- Light microscopy: enlarged, hypercellular glomeruli with obliteration of capillary lumina; diffuse global endocapillary hypercellularity from proliferation of endothelial and mesangial cells plus infiltration of neutrophils and monocytes.
- Immunofluorescence: granular deposits of IgG and C3 along the GBM and in the mesangium, classically described as a starry sky appearance.
- Electron microscopy: subepithelial dome-shaped or hump-shaped electron-dense deposits on the outer side of the GBM (away from the capillary lumen).

## Diagnosis (LO #3)

### History

- Recent infection (strep pharyngitis, impetigo/cellulitis, or staphylococcal infection in adults).

### Urinalysis

- RBCs, dysmorphic RBCs, and RBC casts (all signs of glomerular disease).
- Variable proteinuria, but seldom in the nephrotic range.

### Serum Studies

- Low serum C3 in about 90% of cases (complement consumption); C4 is typically normal because the alternative pathway is activated.
- Decreased GFR with elevated creatinine is uncommon, especially in children.
- Serologic markers of recent strep infection: antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer and Streptozyme test detect antistreptococcal antibodies; useful in PSGN diagnosis.
- Cultures: blood or throat cultures help confirm an underlying streptococcal or staphylococcal infection.

### Clinical Reasoning: C3 vs C4

- PIGN: alternative complement pathway activated by immune complexes -> low C3, normal C4.
- Hereditary angioedema (contrast): loss of C1 esterase inhibitor activates the classical pathway -> low C4.

### Renal Biopsy

- Rarely needed because of typical clinical findings (recent infection, nephritic syndrome, low C3). Indicated if disease does not improve in 1 to 2 weeks, declining GFR, biopsy results would change management, or another glomerular disease is being considered.

## Management (LO #4)

- Mainly supportive in children because the natural course is short.
- Loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide) for fluid removal/edema.
- Blood pressure control as needed.
- Adults with staphylococcal infections require prompt antibiotic therapy; otherwise renal complications can be prolonged.
- Penicillin V is typically used for streptococcal/gram-positive coverage.

## Prognosis

- Most patients (especially children) recover, with symptoms resolving 1 to 2 weeks after presentation.
- In adults, hematuria can persist 3 to 6 months or longer.
- About 1% of children with PIGN progress to renal failure.
- Adults with staphylococcal PIGN have a 25% chance of persistently decreased GFR and a 17% chance of progressing to end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation.

## Summary

- PIGN is an acute nephritic syndrome that follows an extrarenal infection, especially group A Streptococcus (PSGN).
- Most common in children and young adults; staphylococcal PIGN is rising in adults.
- Patients with PIGN typically have a history of recent infectious illness, present with tea or cola-colored urine plus edema/hypertension, and have varied proteinuria.
- Glomerular damage results from immune complex deposition with complement activation (type III hypersensitivity).
- S. pyogenes (group A beta-hemolytic strep) is the classic cause; Staphylococcus species are increasingly recognized.
- Urinalysis: RBCs, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts. Serum: low C3, elevated Streptozyme/ASO titer in PSGN.
- Treatment is supportive in children; antibiotics are essential in adults with staph-related PIGN.
- Children typically recover fully; adults with poststaphylococcal disease have a 17% risk of end-stage kidney disease.
